title: practical english usage - Part 83
content:
I was shocked to see how ill he was.

Jack was surprised to get Anna's message.

I'm pleased to see you. We're happy to be here. She's anxious to go home.

Infinitives are often used after adjectives describing reactions and feelings.

1  reactions       and   feelings:     pleased         to   see    you se  e  you                             __________ 101 infinitives       after    adjectives:      pleased         to In some cases there is a difference of meaning: ►     105,  110.

continue   hate     permit    see begin    go on     love     remember  watch can't bear  go       like     regret    try allow    forget    intend   propose  stop advise   forbid   hear     prefer   start After some verbs, either an -ing form or an infinitive can be used. These include: 4 -ing    form    or  infinitive The car wants servicing. (= ... needs to be serviced.) In informal British English, want can also be used like this.

Your hair needs cutting. (= ... needs to be cut.) I don't think his article deserves reading. (= .. . deserves to be read.) is more common in British than American English.

After deserve, need and require, the -ing form has a passive sense. This structure 3 -ing    form    with    passive      meaning:      Your      hair     needs       cutting.

-ing                                                         Section9 Infinitives,         forms     and   Past   Participles       after    Nouns,    Verbs,   etc grammar »102     infinitives after nouns and pronouns: my decision to leave followed by infinitives (e.g. wish, decide, need).

We   can use infinitives after some nouns which  are related to verbs that can be 1  nouns     related      to  verbs:    no   wish     to   change __    my    de  cision      to   le  ave            ____________ 102 infinitives       after    nouns     and   pronouns: For information about the structures that are possible with a particular adjective, see a good dictionary.

For so + adjective + infinitive (e.g. Would you be so kind as to help me?), ►    584.8.

For more about enough/too + adjective + infinitive, ►    450, 610.

This material can't possibly catch fire, (     l`his-mtiterial is imponsiblr to not Iron rusts easily, (     Iron is easy to rust.) not She has difficulty learning maths, (     She is- difficult to learn math&) of the clause is the subject of the following verb.

Easy, difficult and impossible cannot be used in this structure when the subject not It's a good wine to keep, (     ft's-a good to keep wine.) When the adjective is before a noun, the infinitive is usually after the noun.

She's nice to talk to. (     She^s-nice to talk-to-her-) watch it.) not Cricket is not very interesting to watch, (     Cricket is not very interesting to There is no object pronoun after the infinitive or preposition in these cases.

It’s not a bad place to live in.

She's nice to talk to. He's very easy to get on with.

The  structure often ends with a preposition (►  209.5).

The report is ready for you to check.  The box was too heavy to lift.

Are these berries good to eat? The apples were ripe enough to pick.

His theory is impossible to understand. (= It is impossible to understand his learn Japanese.) Japanese is difficult for Europeans to learn. (= It is difficult for Europeans to or He's easy to please. (= To please him  is easy,  It is easy to please him.) difficult, impossible, good, ready, and adjectives after enough and too.

subject of the clause is really the object of the infinitive. Examples are easy, hard, Some adjectives can be used with infinitives in a special structure, in which  the 4 easy     to   please stay here. Time is not the subject of stay.) not Is this the first time that you have stayed here? (     ... the first-time-for-yowte with the infinitive.

This structure is only possible when the superlative has a subject relationship She's the only scientist to have won three Nobel prizes.

The next to arrive was Mrs Patterson.

Who was the first person to climb Everest without oxygen?

This structure is also common with first, second, third, etc, next, last and only.
